summary: DHH announces that 37signals has completed its exit from the cloud after six months of work, migrating all seven applications—including HEY email and six heritage services—back to their own hardware. The move is projected to save at least $1.5 million per year, with the total hardware investment costing less than a third of one year's savings. They accomplished this using an entirely open-source stack (KVM, Docker, Kamal) while avoiding Kubernetes complexity, and without increasing their operations team size. DHH argues that cloud benefits have been vastly overstated for established companies and urges others to do their own math.
key_points: All seven 37signals applications were migrated off AWS in six months, with the containerization done for cloud actually making the exit easier
The company expects to save $1.5M+ annually, having spent only ~$500K on hardware that will be amortized over five years
The operations team size didn't change—cloud never delivered the promised productivity gains that would allow a smaller team
The open-source stack of KVM, Docker, and Kamal allowed them to avoid both Kubernetes complexity and enterprise vendor lock-in
Cloud still makes sense for early-stage companies and those with highly variable workloads, but established companies are overpaying
money_quotes: The back of the napkin math is that we'll save at least $1.5 million per year by owning our own hardware rather than renting it from Amazon.
Running our applications in the cloud just never provided the promised productivity gains to do with any smaller of a team anyway.
And it was less than a third the cost of what we predict we'll be saving EVERY YEAR!
Just be careful that you don't look at those lavish cloud credits as a gift! It's a hook.
The benefits have been vastly overstated. The cloud is often just as complicated as running things yourself, and it's usually ridiculously more expensive.
If you only need a plough thrice a year, it doesn't make much sense keeping it in the barn unused for the remaining 363 days.
So don't just stay in the cloud because of the hype.
key_insight: For established companies with predictable workloads, owning hardware with open-source tooling is dramatically cheaper than cloud rental, and the cloud's complexity and productivity benefits have been vastly overstated.
